text: The surgeon initiates the procedure with an exploratory laparoscopy, visually inspecting the abdominal cavity to exclude the presence of peritoneal disease.
The surgeon visually identifies the tumor located in the transverse colon, preparing for further surgical intervention.
Prominent lymph nodes are visible overlying the superior mesenteric vessels, and the surgeon identifies the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon encounters the ileocolic pedicle and carefully dissects it to its origin, revealing the anatomical structures and vessels associated with this region.
The surgeon uses a combination of clipping, scissors, and an energy device to divide the ileocolic artery, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the iliocolic vein lying proximal and anterior to the previously dissected structures and ligates it using standard surgical techniques.
The surgeon clips and divides the low-lying middle colic vein, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon provides clear views of the pancreas, highlighting its anatomical position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection, clearly visualizing Henle's trunk. The superior right colic vein and right gastroepiploic vein are ligated using surgical instruments.
The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure, using standard techniques to free the colon from its attachments.
The surgeon uses an energy device to efficiently dissect and manage tissue, enhancing the precision and speed of the procedure.
The surgeon focuses on the ascending colon tumor, which is visibly involved with the abdominal wall.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum, creating an opening in the thin membrane lining the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon angles the energy device cordially to encompass all involved tissue, addressing the advanced nature of the ascending colon tumor with abdominal wall involvement.
The surgeon excises the posterior sheath along with the muscle in a single block, ensuring complete removal of the involved tissue.
The surgeon switches the energy device to the left-hand port to assist in completing the dissection of the anterior abdominal musculature.
The surgeon excises Girotis fascia and perinephric fat on block, along with a partial removal of the right kidney capsule, aiming to achieve negative margins.
The surgeon divides the mesentery along with the specimen, ensuring preservation of the gastropiploic arcade.
The surgeon uses a blue, sixty-millimeter robotic stapler to divide the small bowel, visibly separating the bowel segments with precision.
The surgeon elevates the gastropiploic pedicle while continuing the dissection of the transverse mesocolon, identifying the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs mobilization of the splenic flexure using a hand port and harmonic scalpel, following the de-docking of the robotic system.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein, applies clips to secure it, and then divides the vein using surgical instruments.
After splenic mobilization, the surgical robot is repositioned towards the pelvis, adopting a medial to lateral approach to mobilize the left colon.
The surgeon identifies and skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully preserving the surrounding autonomic nerves during the dissection.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), securing and dividing the vessel with precision.

The surgeon divides the lateral attachments of the colon and continues dissection down to the pelvic floor through the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses the non-operating hand to retract the rectosigmoid anteriorly, enhancing the exposure of the surgical plane for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully preserves the hypogastric nerves, which are visually identified at the sacral promontory, as they descend within the pre-sacral space during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon proceeds with the posterior dissection, placing the rectum on stretch using left bipolar graspers to facilitate further exposure and manipulation.
The surgeon uses traction-counter traction techniques to enhance visibility of the dissection plane during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon follows the curve of the mesorectum to maintain dissection within the embryological plane, carefully avoiding the presacral vessels.
The surgeon visually identifies the mesorectal fascia positioned above and the endopelvic fascia below within the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the anterior lateral aspect, carefully dissecting on the rectal side of Denonvilliers' fascia to preserve the autonomic nerves, considering the patient's young age.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectum, adhering to oncological principles, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon completes the lower posterior dissection, revealing the muscle tube beneath the dissected tissue layers.
The surgeon performs a digital rectal examination to assess the level of the rectum, using gloved fingers to palpate the area and evaluate anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon divides the rectum using two firings of a robotic green 45mm stapler, ensuring precise transection at the assessed level.
The surgeon exteriorizes the colon, bringing it outside the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon creates a 15-centimeter ileal pouch using four firings of the endo-GIA stapler. The ileal pouch anastomosis is then fashioned with the EEA 28-millimeter stapler.

The completed CME (complete mesocolic excision) is visible, showing the iliocolic artery and vein positioned proximally and anteriorly, along with the middle colic vein and artery, the superior right colic vein, and the right gastroepiploic vein.
The camera shifts to the right abdominal wall, revealing a partial peritonectomy where muscle and posterior fascia are resected en bloc, along with visible perinephric fat.
